title: Energy conservation
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master energy conservation for Cambridge 9702 AS Physics — kinetic, potential, work done and efficiency explained with worked examples.
learningObjectives: Apply the principle of conservation of energy to solve problems involving transfers between kinetic energy, gravitational potential energy and work done against resistive forces
Calculate efficiency of energy transfers and express it as a percentage or decimal, identifying where energy is dissipated
Use the equations for kinetic energy (½mv²) and gravitational potential energy (mgh) correctly, including in multi-step problems

simpleExplanation.title: Energy never disappears — it just changes form
simpleExplanation.summary: The total energy of a closed system always stays the same. When a ball falls, its gravitational potential energy converts into kinetic energy. When friction is involved, some energy is transferred as heat — but none is ever created or destroyed.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of energy like money in a bank account: you can move it between accounts (savings, current, investments), but the total balance never changes unless you're charged a fee — which is like friction stealing some energy as heat.
steps: Identify every energy store present at the start and at the end of the situation
Write an energy equation: total energy before = total energy after (+ any energy lost to heat/sound)
Substitute the correct formula for each energy type (½mv² for KE, mgh for GPE) and solve
If efficiency is asked, divide useful output energy by total input energy and multiply by 100%

sections.content: Energy is one of the most fundamental ideas in all of physics — and the good news is that the **principle of conservation of energy** makes every problem solvable. Whether you are dealing with a skier descending a slope, a motor lifting a load, or a ball bouncing off a surface, the rule is always the same: **energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred between stores or dissipated**. Cambridge 9702 tests this idea repeatedly across Paper 1 MCQs and Paper 2 calculations, so a solid grasp of the core equations and how to apply them systematically will earn you marks every single time.
The Principle of Conservation of Energy

The principle states that the **total energy of an isolated system remains constant**. In practice, Cambridge problems always involve some transfer from a 'useful' store (kinetic or gravitational potential energy) into a 'wasted' store (thermal energy due to friction, or sound). Your job is to account for every joule. A complete energy equation looks like this: Initial KE + Initial GPE = Final KE + Final GPE + Work done against resistive forces. Setting up this equation correctly before reaching for a calculator is the single most important exam habit you can build.
Ek = ½mv²   |   Ep = mgh   |   W = Fs cosθ   |   Efficiency = (useful output energy / total input energy) × 100%

items: Gravitational potential energy (GPE) uses the equation Ep = mgh, where h is measured from a clearly defined reference level — always state your reference level in written answers.
Kinetic energy (KE) uses Ek = ½mv²; note the v² term means doubling speed quadruples KE — a favourite trap in Paper 1 MCQs.
Work done by a force equals the energy transferred: W = Fs cosθ. When the force is parallel to displacement, cosθ = 1 and W = Fs.

When friction or air resistance acts, mechanical energy is not conserved — but total energy (including thermal) still is. Distinguish between 'mechanical energy conservation' and 'total energy conservation' in your wording.

Efficiency is always less than 1 (or 100%) for real devices. A question asking you to 'explain why efficiency cannot equal 100%' expects you to state that some energy is always dissipated as thermal energy or sound.
Power is the rate of energy transfer: P = E/t = Fv. This links topic 5.1 directly to topic 5.2 — examiners often combine them in a single Paper 2 question.

sections.content: Worked Example 1 — Roller-coaster speed at the bottom of a slope

sections.question: A roller-coaster carriage of mass 800 kg starts from rest at the top of a slope of vertical height 45 m. The track exerts a constant resistive force of 1200 N on the carriage over its 60 m path down the slope. Calculate the speed of the carriage at the bottom of the slope. (g = 9.81 m s⁻²)
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Identify energy stores.
  • Initial state: carriage at rest at the top → all energy is GPE.
  • Final state: carriage moving at the bottom → KE, with some energy lost to friction.

Step 2 — Write the conservation equation.
  GPE lost = KE gained + Work done against friction
  mgh = ½mv² + Fs

Step 3 — Calculate each term.
  GPE = mgh = 800 × 9.81 × 45 = 353 160 J
  Work done against friction = Fs = 1200 × 60 = 72 000 J

Step 4 — Solve for v.
  ½mv² = 353 160 − 72 000 = 281 160 J
  v² = (2 × 281 160) / 800 = 702.9 m² s⁻²
  v = √702.9 ≈ 26.5 m s⁻¹

Step 5 — State the answer with units.
  Speed at the bottom = 26.5 m s⁻¹ (3 s.f.)

sections.content: Worked Example 2 — Efficiency of an electric motor

sections.question: An electric motor is supplied with 600 J of electrical energy. It raises a 12 kg mass through a vertical height of 4.0 m. Calculate the efficiency of the motor. (g = 9.81 m s⁻²)
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Identify useful output energy.
  Useful output = GPE gained by the mass
  Ep = mgh = 12 × 9.81 × 4.0 = 470.9 J

Step 2 — Apply the efficiency formula.
  Efficiency = useful output energy / total input energy
  Efficiency = 470.9 / 600 = 0.785

Step 3 — Convert to percentage.
  Efficiency = 0.785 × 100% = 78.5%

Note: The remaining 21.5% (≈ 129 J) has been dissipated, primarily as thermal energy in the motor windings and as sound. Always comment on where the 'lost' energy goes if the question asks you to explain.

sections.content: In Paper 2, examiners award a mark specifically for the conservation of energy equation written out symbolically before any numbers are substituted. Even if your arithmetic goes wrong, that method mark is yours for free — so always write the equation first. Similarly, when a question says 'show that' or 'verify', work from known values toward the given answer rather than the other way around, and always write more than the minimum steps so the examiner can see your reasoning.

**Misconception 1 — 'Energy is lost when friction acts.'** Energy is never lost; it is transferred to the surroundings as thermal energy. Use the phrase 'dissipated as thermal energy' rather than 'lost'. Examiners penalise vague language like 'energy disappears'.

**Misconception 2 — Confusing height with distance along slope.** GPE uses the vertical height h, not the length of the slope. In a diagram, look carefully at what is labelled — if you are given the slope length and the angle, calculate h = L sinθ first.

**Misconception 3 — Forgetting initial KE.** If an object starts with a non-zero velocity, its initial KE must appear on the left-hand side of your energy equation. This is a very common source of dropped marks in Paper 1.

items: Always define your reference level for GPE (usually the lowest point in the problem) and state it explicitly.
Check units at every step: energy in joules (J), mass in kg, speed in m s⁻¹, height in m.
For MCQs involving efficiency, eliminate answers above 100% immediately — they are always wrong.
If a question mentions 'smooth' or 'frictionless', set the work-done-against-friction term to zero to simplify the equation.
Percentage efficiency and decimal efficiency are both acceptable unless the question specifies — but make it clear which you are quoting.

faq.q: What equations do I need to know for energy conservation in Cambridge 9702 AS Physics?
faq.a: For Cambridge 9702 topic 5.1, you need three core equations: kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², gravitational potential energy Ep = mgh, and work done W = Fs cosθ. You also need efficiency = useful output energy ÷ total input energy (× 100% for a percentage). Our free premium lesson above covers all of these with worked examples and past-paper style practice so you can apply them confidently under exam conditions.

faq.q: Why do Cambridge mark schemes say 'energy is dissipated' rather than 'energy is lost'?
faq.a: Cambridge examiners follow the principle of conservation of energy strictly — energy is never truly lost, it is simply transferred to a less useful store, most commonly thermal energy in the surroundings due to friction or air resistance. Using the word 'lost' can imply energy is destroyed, which contradicts the principle and can cost you marks. Always write 'dissipated as thermal energy' or 'transferred to the surroundings'. Our free 9702 course reinforces this language throughout, along with mark-scheme practice at /mark?subject=9702&topic=5.1.

faq.q: How do Paper 1 MCQs on energy conservation differ from Paper 2 questions in 9702?
faq.a: Paper 1 MCQs tend to test conceptual understanding and quick calculation — for example, identifying which graph shows KE vs height correctly, or spotting a numerical error in an efficiency calculation. Paper 2 structured questions demand full working, correct units, and often ask you to explain energy transfers in words as well as calculate them. The premium lesson above includes tips specific to both paper styles, and you can practise marking real past-paper responses on our platform to see exactly what examiners reward.
